data.title: Adobe, Block, Ulta, Rubrik And Airbnb: Why These 5 Stocks Are On Investors' Radars Today
data.description:
Major U.S. indices closed lower on Thursday, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average falling 1.56% to 46,677.85, the S&P 500 declining 1.52% to 6,672.62 and the Nasdaq dropping 1.78% to 22,311.97.

These are the top stocks that gained the attention of retail traders and investors through the day:

Adobe Inc. (NASDAQ:ADBE)

Adobe’s stock declined by 1.43% to close at $269.78. The stock reached an intraday high of $276.28 and a low of $269.20, with a 52-week range between $244.28 and $422.95. The stock fell by 7.8% to $248.74 in the after-hours trading.

The company reported first-quarter revenue of $6.40 billion, surpassing analyst estimates of $6.28 billion. Adjusted earnings were $6.06 per share, beating the expected $5.87 per share.

The company guided second-quarter revenue of $6.43 billion to $6.48 billion and adjusted EPS of $5.80 to $5.85, both above estimates. Adobe also reaffirmed full-year revenue guidance of $25.90 billion to $26.10 billion and adjusted EPS of $23.30 to $23.50, while announcing CEO Shantanu Narayen plans to transition from the role once a successor is appointed.

Block Inc. (NYSE:XYZ)

Block’s shares fell 7.29% to $59.90, with an intraday high of $65.07 and a low of $59.74. The stock’s 52-week range is $44.27 to $82.50.

The decline in the shares of the Jack Dorsey-led company was part of a broader sell-off in the technology sector following the release of the Consumer Price Index report, which showed inflation steady at 2.4% in February. 

Ulta Beauty, Inc. (NASDAQ:ULTA)

Ulta’s stock dropped 4.28% to $624.70, with an intraday high of $655.20 and a low of $622.30. The stock’s 52-week range is $309.01 to $714.97. The stock nosedived 8.44% to $572 in the after-hours session.

Ulta Beauty reported fourth-quarter earnings of $8.01 per share and revenue of $3.898 billion, beating analyst estimates of $7.97 per share and $3.802 billion, respectively. Net sales rose 11.8% year over year, while comparable sales increased 5.8%.

For fiscal 2026, the company expects GAAP EPS of $28.05 to $28.55 and revenue of $13.14 billion to $13.26 billion, compared with estimates of $28.38 per share and $13.06 billion. Shares declined in extended trading following the report.

Rubrik, Inc. (NYSE:RBRK)

Rubrik’s shares declined 6.08% to $54.02, with an intraday high of $57.94 and a low of $53.90. The stock’s 52-week range is $46.36 to $103.

Rubrik reported fourth-quarter earnings of 4 cents per share, beating estimates for a loss of 11 cents, while revenue of $377.68 million topped the $342.34 million consensus and rose from $258.1 million a year earlier. Subscription ARR increased 34% year over year to $1.46 billion.

For fiscal 2027, Rubrik expects adjusted earnings of 7 cents to 27 cents per share and revenue of $1.6 billion to $1.61 billion, above the $1.57 billion estimate.

Airbnb Inc. (NASDAQ:ABNB)

Airbnb’s stock fell 4.27% to $127.70, with an intraday high of $133.24 and a low of $126.12. The stock’s 52-week range is $99.89 to $143.88.

The decline was attributed to concerns about global travel demand amid escalating Middle East tensions. 

Benzinga Edge Stock Rankings show that Adobe stock has a Momentum in the 7th percentile and Value in the 25th percentile.

This story was generated using Benzinga Neuro and edited by Shivdeep Dhaliwal

Photo Courtesy: Sudarsan Thobias on Shutterstock.com

data.title: PayPay Lands On Nasdaq — And Cathie Wood Is Already In As Ark Scoops Up $5 Million Worth Of Stock
data.description:
The PayPay Trade

Ark Invest’s ARK Blockchain & Fintech Innovation ETF (BATS:ARKF) fund acquired 275,000 shares of PayPay. This purchase was strategically timed with the IPO, which saw the company offering 54.99 million American depositary shares (ADSs) at $16 per share.

The total value of Ark’s investment in PayPay amounted to nearly $5 million, based on the stock’s closing price of $18.16. The stock popped 13.50% for the day. In the after-hours trading, it gained 4.24% and was seen trading at $18.93.

Other Key Trades:

data.title: Wife, 32, Can't Pay Her 'Share' Of Bills And Is Scared To Tell Hubby Making $250K — 'I Am Not A Wife But A Freeloading Roommate In His Eyes'
data.description:
A household bringing in $250,000 a year usually doesn't include the phrase "I'm broke." But that's exactly how one woman says she feels inside her own marriage.

In a post on Reddit, a 32-year-old woman said she's struggling financially after two layoffs while her 34-year-old husband earns more than $250,000 a year and refuses to combine their finances. The couple has been together nine years and married for one.

Seven years ago she moved from the East Coast to the West Coast so he could work for his father's business and train in a lucrative financial field. She said the move meant leaving behind her family and support system, but she believed it would help build their future together.

Before losing her job, the couple kept their finances separate. Each person covered certain bills, and she sent money toward what she described as her "share" of the household expenses. The mortgage was in his name, but she still contributed while also paying for groceries, the internet bill, dog care and subscriptions.

"I don't consider myself a big spender," she said, explaining that even though she earned less than her husband she always tried to pull her weight financially.

Don't Miss: A single bad hire can set a startup back years. Here are the 5 hires founders most often misjudge — and why

Two Layoffs Changed The Financial Balance

Last year, she was laid off from her job. She briefly found another position, only to lose that one just two months later.

Months into her search for new work, she said her husband still wants to keep finances separate until she secures another job. Meanwhile, she says her own financial situation has steadily deteriorated.

Savings have been drained. She sold stock to cover expenses that used to be routine. She now estimates she carries about $12,000 in credit card debt.

"I pay only for the essentials and every time I use my credit card I have a mini panic attack it's going to be declined for buying eggs and bread," she said.

Other setbacks made things harder. Her car was totaled shortly after the first layoff, making it more difficult to travel for job opportunities.

To improve her prospects she completed an additional certification. She has also taken on dog sitting jobs to bring in some extra cash while she continues searching for full-time work.

Financial help from her parents has played a role in keeping her afloat. She said they gift her about $30,000 per year, though she suspects that outside support may be one reason her husband doesn't fully grasp how strained her finances have become.

The couple also tried to offset the missing income by renting out a room in their home to one of her husband's friends.

Still, she says the imbalance has left her feeling increasingly isolated.

Trending: From college to emergencies, these money rules could make or break your family's security

Marriage expectations spark debate

Much of the discussion around her story focused on what financial partnership should look like inside a marriage.

Several commenters pointed out that she had been contributing toward a mortgage tied only to her husband's name. Others questioned the broader dynamic.

One person wrote that the situation raised questions about the meaning behind traditional vows such as "for richer or poorer."

Another commenter said the arrangement made the relationship sound less like a marriage and more like two roommates dividing expenses.

Some responses were even more blunt. One commenter suggested that if the dynamic doesn't change, she might receive more financial support through a divorce than she currently does within the marriage.

Others focused on the detail that she receives $30,000 annually from her parents, questioning whether that outside financial cushion may have delayed a more serious conversation between the couple.

Despite the disagreements over details, many readers landed on the same theme: marriage typically involves helping a partner through temporary setbacks.

A conversation she still hasn't had

The woman said the most difficult part of the situation isn't just the debt or the job search. It's figuring out how to talk to her husband about it.

"I frankly feel rather stupid that I don't know how to communicate my needs which are to simply have a joint checking account and to help me until I secure full work," she said.

Her husband, she added, says he has about $400,000 in savings and brings in roughly $20,000 a month.

The thought of raising the issue still makes her anxious.

"I feel like if I talk to him and he's not on board at this point I am not a wife but a freeloading roommate in his eyes," she said.

Money disagreements are one of the most common sources of tension in relationships. Some couples maintain separate finances for years, while others combine everything from the start.

Financial planners often say the key isn't which system a couple chooses, but whether both partners clearly understand the expectations. When disagreements grow complicated, some couples turn to a financial advisor to help create a shared plan.

For this woman, the next step may simply be starting the conversation she has been avoiding. After months of layoffs, mounting debt and a difficult job market, she says she is still trying to figure out how to ask her husband for help without risking the marriage itself.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: $100 Invested In BlackRock 20 Years Ago Would Be Worth This Much Today
data.description:
BlackRock (NYSE:BLK) has outperformed the market over the past 20 years by 1.38% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 9.91%. Currently, BlackRock has a market capitalization of $143.57 billion.

Buying $100 In BLK: If an investor had bought $100 of BLK stock 20 years ago, it would be worth $659.26 today based on a price of $925.67 for BLK at the time of writing.

BlackRock's Performance Over Last 20 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.
